    14. Appendix: /grill-with-docs

    Appendix: /grill-with-docs

    When working with an agent on the same project repeatedly, you start to encounter a consistent problem. To understand it, we need to look at the sources of information available to agents when they're working in your codebase.

    Information Sources Available to Agents

    An agent typically has access to four main sources of information:

    1. Current Code - The current commit of code that's accessible to the agent
    2. Commit History - Historical information about how the code has evolved, allowing the agent to understand changes, find bugs, or track design decisions
    3. GitHub/Issue Trackers - External information accessible via the GitHub CLI or other integrated tools (especially useful when commit messages reference issue numbers)
    4. Your Messages - The instructions and context you provide directly

    This all sounds comprehensive until you realize there are two critical questions that agents struggle to answer using only these sources.

    The Questions Agents Can't Easily Answer

    Why Did You Do It This Way?

    The first difficult question is: why did you do it this way?

    When there's an unusual trade-off visible in the code, agents need to understand:

    • Why was this approach chosen over alternatives?
    • Why was one technology selected instead of another?
    • What constraints or considerations led to this decision?

    An agent might theoretically find this information by digging through commit history and GitHub issues, but in practice, it's extremely difficult for them to piece together. Yet this understanding is crucial, because without it, the agent might recommend repeating the same pattern unnecessarily.

    Architectural Decision Records (ADRs)

    The solution many engineering teams use is Architectural Decision Records (ADRs). These are markdown files that document important design decisions, explaining:

    • Why a decision was made
    • What alternatives were considered
    • Any relevant context or constraints

    Because ADRs live in your codebase, they're immediately accessible to any agent working there. This gives the agent quick, direct answers to questions about your design choices.

    The Second Challenge: Shared Language

    However, ADRs alone can't solve the second difficult question: what on earth is an XXX?

    In other words, you're using domain-specific jargon that the agent simply doesn't understand. This isn't a flaw in the agent - it's specialized language that makes perfect sense within your business context.

    A Real Example

    Consider a video management application. It might use terms like:

    • Videos for lessons vs videos for YouTube
    • Pitches (which can have attached videos, or be standalone)
    • Ghost lessons and ghost sections (lessons/sections planned but not yet created)
    • Materialization cascade (the chain reaction that occurs when a planned lesson becomes real, converting ghost sections into actual sections)

    These terms are convenient shorthand for you, but an agent has no context for them. You end up having to explain them repeatedly in every interaction.

    Creating a Shared Glossary

    The solution is to maintain a shared glossary - a CONTEXT.md file at the root of your repository that defines your domain-specific terminology.

    Here's a real example from a course video manager:

    # Context and Glossary

    

    ## Course Structure

    

    - **Course**: A collection of lessons organized together

    - **Section**: A grouping of lessons within a course

    - **Ghost Section**: A section that's planned but not yet materialized

    - **Lesson**: Individual learning content

    - **Ghost Lesson**: A lesson planned for a course but not yet created

    

    ## Content Types

    

    - **Video**: Media content, can be attached to lessons, YouTube, or pitches

    - **Pitch**: A marketing or proposal video that can have associated videos

    

    ## Key Concepts

    

    - **Materialization Cascade**: The chain reaction when converting a ghost lesson into a real lesson, which materializes its ghost section into a real section

    With this glossary in place, you can simply say "the materialization cascade" instead of explaining the entire concept each time. The agent can refer to precise definitions as needed.

    Domain-Driven Design Connection

    This approach aligns with the concept of ubiquitous language from Domain-Driven Design. A ubiquitous language is shared between:

    • Domain experts (the people you're building for)
    • Developers
    • The codebase itself (through clear naming)

    When you establish this shared language, it provides multiple benefits:

    • The agent can communicate with you more effectively
    • Variable naming becomes more intuitive
    • Finding relevant code in the codebase becomes easier
    • The entire team operates with consistent terminology

    When to Create This Documentation

    The timing of when you create ADRs and glossaries matters. You might initially think to create them after writing product requirements, but this creates a problem: all the issues in your documentation will use old domain language.

    Instead, create this documentation at the earliest possible moment - during your initial grilling session.

    The Ideal Workflow

    1. Grill with the agent - Have your initial planning conversation
    2. Hammer out the glossary - Define any new domain terms that emerge
    3. Create ADRs - Document any architectural decisions made during planning
    4. Commit to the repository - Add these files so they're accessible going forward
    5. Write PRDs - Create product requirement documents on top of this foundation

    This ensures that everything downstream uses the correct, shared language.

    Automating This Process with Grill with Docs

    The challenge with the workflow above is that it requires you to remember to create ADRs and update your glossary every single time. Fortunately, there's a skill for this: /grill-with-docs.

    /grill-with-docs is essentially a drop-in replacement for the standard grilling skill. It runs a complete grilling session while simultaneously:

    • Maintaining your CONTEXT.md glossary
    • Creating ADRs for significant architectural decisions

    Using Grill with Docs

    You can now use /grill-with-docs as a drop-in replacement for your standard grilling workflow. The typical usage pattern is:

    • Use /grill-with-docs whenever you're working on code
    • Use the standard /grill-me skill when working on non-code projects

    This ensures that every codebase you work in has the documentation your agent needs to answer the toughest questions.

    Conclusion

    By combining Architectural Decision Records and a shared glossary, you create an environment where agents can:

    • Understand why decisions were made
    • Understand what your domain-specific terms mean
    • Operate more effectively within your codebase

    /grill-with-docs automates the process of creating and maintaining this documentation, making it a practical addition to every development workflow.
